perturbation.original_text: SUPPLIER agrees to hold all Confidential Information in strict confidence and will not disclose or use the Confidential Information for its benefit or the benefit of any other company or entity anywhere in the world or any other purpose other than for the Purpose set forth in the recitals herein.
perturbation.changed_text: SUPPLIER agrees to use commercially reasonable efforts to hold all Confidential Information in confidence and will endeavor not to disclose or use the Confidential Information for its direct benefit or the direct benefit of any other company or entity anywhere in the world or for any other primary purpose other than for the Purpose set forth in the recitals herein.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates 'strict confidence,' a clear obligation. Modifying this to 'commercially reasonable efforts' introduces ambiguity and weakens the protection of Confidential Information. Similarly, the addition of 'direct benefit' and 'primary purpose' provides loopholes for potentially permissible uses or disclosures, blurring the lines of what is prohibited.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets when reasonable efforts are not enough to prevent their disclosure. The original NDA mandates 'strict confidence,' offering stronger protection aligned with trade secret law. The modified language, allowing for 'commercially reasonable efforts,' could potentially lead to inadequate protection if those efforts fall short, thereby undermining CUTSA’s aim to safeguard trade secrets effectively.

(b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, including but not limited to disclosures made under section 1-210, sections 31-40j to 31-40p, inclusive, or subsection (c) of section 12-62; or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that i
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'strict confidence,' which provides a high level of protection for confidential information. The changed text introduces 'commercially reasonable efforts,' which weakens this protection. According to the Law Explanation, this change could lead to inadequate protection of trade secrets, potentially undermining the aim of CUTSA (presumably the Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act). The introduction of 'direct benefit' and 'primary purpose' further weakens the obligation by creating loopholes for disclosure. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing Connecticut General Statutes § 35-51, defines 'misappropriation' and includes 'breach or inducement of a breach of duty to maintain secrecy.' By weakening the duty to maintain strict confidence to merely using 'commercially reasonable efforts,' the modified contract increases the risk of misappropriation, creating a potential legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: SUPPLIER shall maintain all Confidential Information received from GK in the same manner that the SUPPLIER maintains its own Confidential Information, provided that the standard of care required shall be at least a reasonable standard.
perturbation.changed_text: SUPPLIER shall endeavor to maintain Confidential Information received from GK in a manner they deem appropriate for their own Confidential Information, acknowledging that the standard of care should ideally be reasonable.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a clear benchmark: maintaining GK's confidential information with at least a 'reasonable standard' equivalent to their own. The modified version introduces subjectivity by stating 'in a manner they deem appropriate,' and weakens the 'reasonable standard' to 'should ideally be reasonable,' reducing the level of protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The modified language, allowing the Supplier to determine what is 'appropriate,' could result in a lower standard of care than what is legally required to maintain trade secret protection under Connecticut law. 'Deeming appropriate' might not always align with what is 'reasonable' in a legal context, jeopardizing trade secret status.

(b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, including but not limited to disclosures made under section 1-210, sections 31-40j to 31-40p, inclusive, or subsection (c) of section 12-62; or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that i
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the standard of care required to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting CUTSA's requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The original text mandated a 'reasonable standard,' while the changed text uses subjective terms like 'deem appropriate' and softens the 'reasonable standard' to 'should ideally be reasonable.' This shift could allow the SUPPLIER to apply a lower standard of care than legally required. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of duty to maintain secrecy,' which is potentially facilitated by the lower standard permitted in the modified contract. The change introduces ambiguity around the required level of protection, making it difficult to enforce a consistent standard of care and potentially jeopardizing trade secret status under CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: Any disclosure of Confidential Inform ation shall be limited SUPPLIER’s employees and who have a need to use or study such Confidential Information for the Purpose. SUPPLIER shall advise its employees and agen ts of its obligations pursuant to this Agreement, but SUPPLIER shall remain responsible to ensure their compliance.
perturbation.changed_text: Any disclosure of Confidential Information should generally be limited to SUPPLIER’s employees who might potentially benefit from using or studying such Confidential Information for aspects related to the Purpose. SUPPLIER will inform its employees and agents of this Agreement and make efforts to encourage their compliance.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause clearly limits disclosure to employees with a 'need to use or study' the information and makes the supplier responsible for ensuring compliance. By changing 'shall be limited' to 'should generally be limited,' 'need to use or study' to 'might potentially benefit from using or studying,' and removing the assurance of compliance, the clause becomes less restrictive and enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The stricter the controls a company has over its confidential information, the better it is protected under CUTSA. The new language provides a weaker control over who can access the data. By only providing suggestions to keep the data private, rather than enforcing that standard, it weakens the 'reasonable' standards of CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 35-51. Definitions. :: Chapter 625 — Uniform Trade Secrets Act (contains Secs. 35-50 to 35-58) :: Title 35 — Trade Regulations, Trademarks and Collective and Certification Marks (contains Chapters 620 to 625) :: 2005 Connecticut Code :: Connecticut Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Connecticut General Statutes 2024 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2023 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2022 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2005 Conn. Gen. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 Connecticut Code - Sec. 35-51. Definitions. Sec. 35-51. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means, including searching through trash. (b) "Misappropriation" means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, including but not limited to disclosures made under section 1-210, sections 31-40j to 31-40p, inclusive, or subsection (c) of section 12-62; or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, limited liability com
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the control over confidential information, potentially weakening its protection under trade secret laws. The original text mandates limitations on disclosure ('shall be limited') and ensures compliance, while the modified text uses softer language ('should generally be limited') and encourages compliance rather than ensuring it. According to the Law Explanation, the stricter the controls a company has over its confidential information, the better it is protected under CUTSA. The original language provides a stronger degree of control, whereas the altered language provides a weaker control over who has access to the data, and how they use it. This is further substantiated by Scraped Law Snippet 1 which states that 'Misappropriation' includes disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret, or at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. By weakening the obligation to maintain secrecy or limit its use, the modified contract creates an ambiguity making it difficult to enforce obligations.

perturbation.original_text: At any time that GKT may request, SUPPLIER shall return the Confidential Information to GK and shall certify in writing that all copies thereof in its possession have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon reasonable request from GKT, SUPPLIER will use good faith efforts to return most of the Confidential Information to GK, and will provide a statement that, to the best of their knowledge, all readily accessible copies thereof in their direct possession have been addressed.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces several ambiguities. 'Reasonable request' is subjective. 'Good faith efforts' does not guarantee complete return of information. The phrase 'most of the Confidential Information' leaves room for keeping some information. Restricting the destruction certification to 'readily accessible copies' and 'direct possession' creates loopholes.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, which includes proper control over trade secrets and ensuring that they are not disclosed without authorization. This means upon the request of the owner of the trade secret, the data should be returned so that it is no longer exposed. By making the return vague and allowing for the potential retention of data, it reduces the likelihood of CUTSA protection.
perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates that the SUPPLIER shall return all Confidential Information and certify its destruction, whereas the changed text weakens this obligation significantly. The changed text only requires 'good faith efforts' to return 'most' of the information and provides a statement to the 'best of their knowledge' about 'readily accessible copies' in their 'direct possession.' The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight how this change introduces ambiguities and loopholes. Although there are no law snippets available to support this claim, the weakening of the obligation is significant enough to create a legal contradiction, especially regarding maintaining secrecy and proper control, as the LLM suggests would be necessary for CUTSA protection. The original text aimed for complete return and certified destruction, while the changed version allows for potential retention and uncertainty. This clearly undermines the original intent to protect confidential information.
